2:
"On the first day of the first month shalt thou set up the tabernacle of the tent of the congregation." The witnesses note the command to rear the tabernacle on the first day of the first month — the crux of the dwelling of God set up. On "On the first day of the first month shalt thou set up the tabernacle," interpreters expound the day. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the time — "on the first day of the first month shalt thou set up the tabernacle of the tent of the congregation": the tabernacle was to be reared on the first day of the first month (Abib/Nisan), the beginning of the second year after the Exodus (vv.17); the witnesses note the significance — the new beginning (the dwelling of God set up at the turn of the year, the first day, the new start, almost a year after the Exodus, Exodus 12:2; 19:1); the dwelling of God among men (the tabernacle the dwelling-place of God in the midst of his people, the great purpose, "let them make me a sanctuary; that I may dwell among them," Exodus 25:8); the figure — God coming to dwell with man, the tabernacle a figure of the incarnation ("the Word was made flesh, and dwelt [tabernacled] among us," John 1:14), and of God's dwelling with his people, the church (Ephesians 2:21-22) and the consummation (Revelation 21:3, "the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them"); the new creation (the dwelling reared at the new year, the figure of the new beginning, God dwelling with man).

On "the tabernacle of the tent of the congregation," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the dwelling. Wesley and Keil note the tent of the congregation (the place of meeting, where God met his people) — the meeting-place (God dwelling and meeting with his people); the figure (the dwelling and meeting of God with man, fulfilled in Christ).

On the rearing, the grammatical-historical note the setting up: the tabernacle set up on the first day — the dwelling reared; the providence (the dwelling of God set up, the figure of God coming to dwell with man). The verse displays the command to rear the tabernacle on the first day of the first month.

The witnesses agree the substance: the tabernacle was to be reared on the first day of the first month, the beginning of the second year (Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill). The witnesses agree the substance: the cloud by day and fire by night rested on the tabernacle, visible to all Israel, through all their journeys (Calvin, Clarke, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the abiding presence and guidance: "the cloud of the LORD was upon the tabernacle by day, and fire... by night... throughout all their journeys" — the abiding presence of God with his pilgrim people, both guide and assurance, day and night, to the promised land (Exodus 13:21-22; Numbers 9:15-16), a figure of God's constant presence with his church ("I will never leave thee," Hebrews 13:5; Matthew 28:20) and the Lord the everlasting light and guide of his people (Isaiah 60:19-20; Revelation 21:23). The verse displays the abiding-presence crux. The cloud by day and fire by night through all their journeys display the crux: the cloud by day and fire by night, throughout all their journeys (Exodus 13:21-22; Hebrews 13:5; Matthew 28:20; Revelation 21:23) — the abiding presence of God dwelling with and guiding his redeemed people, the book of redemption closing on the assured, constant presence of God with his church. The verse closes the book of Exodus, the dwelling of God among his people established and his presence abiding.
